Imagine you have a rowdy classroom of students. On one side of the room, you have the "Capacitor" kids who always want to pull the class schedule completely forward. On the other side, you have the "Inductor" kids who always want to push the class schedule completely backward. Usually, the classroom is chaotic. But what happens if both groups pull with the exact same strength? They cancel each other out completely, and the classroom runs perfectly smoothly, as if neither group was there! 

In an Alternating Current (AC) LCR circuit, the inductor and capacitor provide opposing types of "electrical resistance" called Reactance. The inductor creates Inductive Reactance (X_L) and the capacitor creates Capacitive Reactance (X_C). 
When an AC circuit reaches a magical state called "Resonance," it means the frequency of the electricity is tuned perfectly so that X_L is exactly equal to X_C! 

Because they point in opposite directions mathematically, they completely annihilate each other. It's like a tug-of-war where both sides are equally strong—the rope doesn't move left or right!
So, if the inductor and capacitor cancel each other out, what is left in the circuit? Only the normal, boring Resistor! 

The problem states we are "at resonance." This is the golden keyword! It means we can entirely ignore whatever inductors or capacitors are in the circuit. The total "Impedance" (which is the grand total of all electrical resistance in an AC circuit) simply becomes equal to the normal resistance.
So, Impedance (Z) = Resistance (R) = 55 \Omega. 

Now, finding the current is incredibly easy. We just use good old Ohm's Law (Current = Voltage / Resistance). The AC source provides an average (RMS) voltage of 110 V. 
Current (I_{rms}) = \frac{V_{rms}}{Z} = \frac{110 \text{ Volts}}{55 \Omega} = 2 Amperes. 

And that's it! Because the circuit was in resonance, the complex AC problem melted down into the simplest math equation possible. A steady 2 Amps of current will bravely flow right through that 55 \Omega resistor!

questions.Concept: Heating Effect of AC Current and Power Dissipation
questions.Question: 5. An AC source is connected to inductance 100 mH, capacitance 100 μF and resistance 120 Ω. The resistance has thermal capacity 2 J/°C. Find the time in which it will get heated by 16°C.
MCQ_Options: Dependent on the RMS voltage of the source (Requires source voltage to calculate)
questions.Hint: Remember that heat energy is calculated as Thermal Capacity multiplied by the change in temperature, and electrical power is the square of RMS current multiplied by resistance.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Hello there, young physicist! Let us tackle this really cool real-world application of Alternating Current circuits! Have you ever wondered how an electric room heater or a toaster actually gets hot? It all comes down to the friction-like resistance that electrons experience when pushing through a wire. When they bump into atoms, they release energy as heat! 

Let us break this problem into two beautiful steps. First, let us look at the heat required. The problem tells us the resistor has a 'thermal capacity' of 2 J/\circ C. Thermal capacity is just a fancy way of saying: 'To make this object 1 degree hotter, I need to feed it 2 Joules of energy.' We want to heat it by 16\circ C. So, the total heat energy (Q) we need is simply: Q = Thermal Capacity \times \Delta T = 2 \times 16 = 32 Joules.

Now, where does this heat come from? It comes from the electrical power of the AC source! The power dissipated by a resistor is given by P = I_{rms}^2 \times R, and the total energy delivered over time 't' is Energy = P \times t = I_{rms}^2 \times R \times t. 

Here is the catch: the original problem text forgot to give us the voltage or the current of the AC source! Without knowing how much current is flowing, we mathematically cannot find the exact time. But as true physicists, we focus on the method! If, for example, the circuit had an RMS current of 0.5 Amps, the power would be P = (0.5)^2 \times 120 = 0.25 \times 120 = 30 Watts (which is 30 Joules per second). To get 32 Joules, the time would be t = 32 / 30 = 1.06 seconds. Always remember the core principle: Electrical Energy Dissipated = Heat Energy Absorbed. You are doing amazing, keep this concept locked in your brain!
questions.Answer: Dependent on the RMS voltage of the source

questions.Question: 6. In the given circuit, |V_L| and |V_C| are twice |V_R|. Given f = 50 Hz, inductance of the coil is 1/(Kπ) mH. Find K.
MCQ_Options: Cannot be determined without the resistance value
questions.Hint: When the voltage across the inductor equals the voltage across the capacitor, the circuit is operating at resonance!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Welcome back, detective! We have a fascinating mystery here with a missing circuit diagram, but we have enough clues to uncover some brilliant physics secrets! 

The problem tells us a massive clue: the magnitude of voltage across the inductor |V_L| and the voltage across the capacitor |V_C| are BOTH equal to twice the voltage across the resistor |V_R|. Because |V_L| = |V_C|, our AC circuit is in a magical state called 'Resonance'! 

Imagine pushing a friend on a swing. If you push at random times, they don't go very high. But if you push at exactly the right rhythm—matching their natural frequency—they swing incredibly high! That is resonance. In an electrical circuit, resonance happens when the 'pushback' from the inductor (Inductive Reactance, X_L) perfectly cancels out the 'pushback' from the capacitor (Capacitive Reactance, X_C). 

Now, we are also told |V_L| = 2|V_R|. Using Ohm's Law for AC circuits (V = I \times X), this means I \times X_L = 2 \times I \times R. The currents cancel out, leaving us with X_L = 2R. We know that X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L. 

So, 2\pi(50) L = 2R, which simplifies to 100\pi L = 2R. To find the inductance L, we need the resistance R. Since the original diagram is missing the value of R, we can express the answer in terms of R! L = 2R / (100\pi) = R / (50\pi) Henrys. The problem states L = 1/(K\pi) mH. To find the exact number 'K', we would simply plug in the value of R if we had it. The beautiful lesson here is recognizing the resonance condition and using voltage ratios to link reactance and resistance. You are learning to think exactly like an engineer!
questions.Answer: Cannot be determined without the resistance value

questions.Concept: Impedance vs Frequency in LCR Circuits
questions.Question: 7. For a series LCR circuit, I vs ω curve is shown. Which statements about the circuit being capacitive/inductive and impedance at resonance are correct?
MCQ_Options: Capacitive for ω < ω_r, Inductive for ω > ω_r, Impedance is minimum at resonance
Inductive for ω < ω_r, Capacitive for ω > ω_r, Impedance is maximum at resonance
Capacitive for ω < ω_r, Inductive for ω > ω_r, Impedance is maximum at resonance
Inductive for ω < ω_r, Capacitive for ω > ω_r, Impedance is minimum at resonance
questions.Hint: At low frequencies, a capacitor acts like a giant roadblock (high reactance). At high frequencies, an inductor becomes the giant roadblock. What happens to total resistance (impedance) when they cancel each other out?
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Hey there! Imagine you are driving on a very strange highway where your speed (frequency, \omega) changes the size of the speed bumps. In an AC circuit, we have two types of 'speed bumps': Capacitors and Inductors. 

For a Capacitor, its resistance to alternating current is called Capacitive Reactance: X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}. Notice that \omega is in the denominator! This means if you drive very slowly (low frequency, \omega < \omega_r), the capacitor acts like a massive wall. It dominates the circuit. Therefore, at low frequencies, the circuit is heavily 'Capacitive'.

For an Inductor, its resistance is Inductive Reactance: X_L = \omega L. Here, \omega is in the numerator! If you drive incredibly fast (high frequency, \omega > \omega_r), the inductor becomes the massive wall. It dominates the circuit. So, at high frequencies, the circuit is 'Inductive'.

Now, what happens exactly at the magic speed, the resonant frequency (\omega_r)? At this sweet spot, the capacitor's roadblock perfectly cancels out the inductor's roadblock (X_L = X_C). They destroy each other's effects! The only thing left slowing down the current is the plain old Resistor. Because these two big roadblocks cancel out, the total overall roadblock (Impedance, Z) is at its absolute lowest (minimum). And because the impedance is lowest, the current 'I' shoots up to its maximum peak! This is why the correct option perfectly describes the nature of the circuit across different frequencies.
questions.Answer: Capacitive for ω < ω_r, Inductive for ω > ω_r, Impedance is minimum at resonance

questions.Concept: High Frequency Behavior of AC Components
questions.Question: 8. Find the effective current I in the given circuit at very high frequencies.
MCQ_Options: Calculated by treating inductors as open circuits and capacitors as short circuits
Calculated by treating inductors as short circuits and capacitors as open circuits
Zero, because high frequency blocks all current
Infinite, because high frequency pushes current too fast
questions.Hint: As frequency (ω) approaches infinity, what happens to the mathematical values of X_L (ωL) and X_C (1/ωC)?
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let us dive into a superpower that physics gives you: the ability to look at extreme situations! The question asks what happens to a circuit at 'very high frequencies'. Let us pretend the frequency (\omega) is basically infinity. It is vibrating so fast it is practically a blur! 

How do our components react to this super-speed? Let us look at the Inductor first. Its reactance (pushback) is X_L = \omega L. If \omega becomes infinitely huge, X_L also becomes infinitely huge! An infinitely huge resistance means absolutely zero current can pass through. In circuit terms, we call this an 'Open Circuit'—it is as if someone took scissors and cut the wire where the inductor was! 

Now look at the Capacitor. Its reactance is X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}. If \omega becomes infinitely huge, we are dividing 1 by a massive number, which means X_C becomes practically zero! A component with zero resistance is just a plain, perfect wire. We call this a 'Short Circuit'.

So, if you are ever given a complex circuit diagram and asked to find the current at very high frequencies, you can use this amazing trick: take your pencil, erase every single inductor (leaving a broken gap), and replace every single capacitor with a solid straight line! Then, you just use good old Ohm's Law (I = V / R_{equivalent}) with whatever resistors are left connected to the battery. It turns a scary AC problem into a super simple DC-style problem. You've got this!
questions.Answer: Calculated by treating inductors as open circuits and capacitors as short circuits

questions.Concept: Voltage and Current Phase Relationship in Inductors
questions.Question: 9. The equation of current in a purely inductive circuit is 5 sin(49πt - 30°). Inductance is 30 mH. Find the equation for voltage across the inductor.
questions.Hint: In a purely inductive circuit, voltage always leads the current by exactly 90 degrees (or π/2 radians). Calculate the peak voltage using V_0 = I_0 × X_L.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Alright, my friend, imagine an inductor as a very stubborn, heavy spinning wheel. It hates sudden changes! If you try to change its speed, it fights back. Mathematically, the voltage across an inductor is given by the rate of change of current: V = L \frac{dI}{dt}. 

Let us use the simpler, highly visual method. We know a golden rule in AC physics: in a purely inductive circuit, the Voltage ALWAYS leads the Current by 90\circ (which is \frac{\pi}{2} radians). This happens because the inductor fights the current, so the voltage has to push hard before the current actually starts moving. 

First, let us find the peak voltage (V_0). We know V_0 = I_0 \times X_L. 
The equation for current is I = 5 \sin(49\pi t - 30\circ). 
The peak current I_0 is the number in front, which is 5 Amperes. 
The frequency term \omega is the number next to 't', which is 49\pi rad/s. 
The inductance L is given as 30 mH, which is 30 \times 10^{-3} Henrys. 

Let us calculate the inductor's resistance (Reactance, X_L): 
X_L = \omega L = 49\pi \times 30 \times 10^{-3} = 1.47\pi \Omega. 
Now, peak voltage V_0 = I_0 \times X_L = 5 \times 1.47\pi = 7.35\pi Volts. 

Now for the phase shift! Since voltage leads current by 90\circ, we take the inside of the current's sine wave and just add 90\circ to it! 
The current phase is (49\pi t - 30\circ). 
The voltage phase will be (49\pi t - 30\circ + 90\circ) = (49\pi t + 60\circ). 

Put it all together: V = V_0 \sin(\text{new phase}) = 7.35\pi \sin(49\pi t + 60\circ). You just mastered phase shifts and reactance all in one go!

questions.Question: 10. An alternating emf E = 440 sin 100πt is applied to an inductance of √2/π H. An AC ammeter is connected. Find its reading.
questions.Hint: An AC ammeter does not read the peak current; it reads the RMS (Root Mean Square) current. Find the peak current first, then divide by √2.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let us solve a mystery involving an AC Ammeter! If you hook up a regular old DC ammeter to an alternating current, the needle will just vibrate at zero because the current keeps switching directions. To measure AC, we use special AC ammeters, and here is the golden secret: they ALWAYS read the RMS (Root Mean Square) value, not the peak value. The RMS value is the 'effective' value that would do the same amount of heating as a DC current.

Let us extract our clues from the voltage equation: E = 440 \sin(100\pi t). 
The number in front is our Peak Voltage, E_0 = 440 V. 
The number next to 't' is our angular frequency, \omega = 100\pi rad/s. 
The inductance L is given as \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi} Henrys. 

Step 1: Find how much the inductor fights the current. This is the Inductive Reactance (X_L). 
X_L = \omega \times L = 100\pi \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi}. The \pi on top and bottom beautifully cancel out, leaving us with X_L = 100\sqrt{2} \Omega. 

Step 2: Find the Peak Current (I_0). Just like standard Ohm's law (I = V/R), for AC we use I_0 = E_0 / X_L. 
I_0 = 440 / (100\sqrt{2}) = 4.4 / \sqrt{2} Amperes. 

Step 3: Convert Peak Current to what the ammeter actually reads, the RMS Current. 
The formula is I_{rms} = \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}. 
So, I_{rms} = \frac{4.4 / \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4.4}{2} = 2.2 Amperes. 

The ammeter will comfortably sit at 2.2 A! You did a fantastic job tracking those numbers step-by-step.

questions.Concept: Series Combination of Elements and Impedance
questions.Question: 11. A circuit element X when connected to AC supply of peak voltage 100 V gives peak current 5 A in phase. Element Y gives same peak current lagging by π/2. If X and Y are connected in series to the same supply, find the rms current.
questions.Hint: Identify the elements: 'In phase' means a resistor, 'lagging by π/2' means an inductor. Find their individual resistances (R and X_L), calculate the total series impedance (Z), find the new peak current, and convert to RMS.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's put on our detective hats! The problem gives us clues about two mystery elements, X and Y. 

Clue 1: Element X gives a current that is 'in phase' with the voltage. When voltage and current dance together in perfect sync, that is the undeniable signature of a plain old Resistor! 
Its resistance R = Voltage / Current = 100 V / 5 A = 20 \Omega.

Clue 2: Element Y gives a current that 'lags by \pi/2' (which is 90 degrees). Remember our stubborn spinning wheel? An inductor fights the change, so current is sluggish and falls behind the voltage. Element Y is an Inductor! 
Its reactance X_L = Voltage / Current = 100 V / 5 A = 20 \Omega.

Now, the big finale! We connect X (Resistor) and Y (Inductor) in series to the same 100 V peak supply. How do their resistances combine? Because they are out of phase by 90 degrees, we cannot just add 20 + 20. We have to use the Pythagorean theorem to find the total Impedance (Z)! 
Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{400 + 400} = \sqrt{800} = 20\sqrt{2} \Omega.

Now we find the new peak current (I_0) for this combined circuit: 
I_0 = Peak Voltage / Total Impedance = 100 / (20\sqrt{2}) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} Amperes.

But wait! The question asks for the RMS current, not the peak. 
The formula to convert is I_{rms} = \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}. 
So, I_{rms} = \frac{5/\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 Amperes. 
Brilliant deduction from start to finish! You identified the components, merged their behaviors, and found the real-world reading.

questions.Concept: Nature of LCR Circuits Based on Reactance
questions.Question: 12. Match List-I with List-II for conditions ωL > 1/ωC, ωL = 1/ωC, ωL < 1/ωC, and resonant frequency.
MCQ_Options: ωL > 1/ωC : Inductive | ωL = 1/ωC : Resistive | ωL < 1/ωC : Capacitive
ωL > 1/ωC : Capacitive | ωL = 1/ωC : Resistive | ωL < 1/ωC : Inductive
ωL > 1/ωC : Resistive | ωL = 1/ωC : Inductive | ωL < 1/ωC : Capacitive
ωL > 1/ωC : Inductive | ωL = 1/ωC : Capacitive | ωL < 1/ωC : Resistive
questions.Hint: Look at which component has the larger value. The component with the higher reactance 'wins' and dictates the overall behavior of the circuit.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's play a matching game with AC circuits! In a series LCR circuit, we have three friends sharing a path: the Resistor (R), the Inductor (L), and the Capacitor (C). But the Inductor and Capacitor are like two people pulling a rope in opposite directions. The one who pulls harder decides where the rope goes!

Let's analyze the forces:
The 'pull' of the Inductor is its reactance: X_L = \omega L.
The 'pull' of the Capacitor is its reactance: X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}.

Scenario 1: \omega L > \frac{1}{\omega C}. This means X_L > X_C. The Inductor is pulling harder! Because the inductor dominates the circuit, the overall circuit behaves like an inductor. We call this an 'Inductive' circuit. The current will lag behind the voltage.

Scenario 2: \omega L < \frac{1}{\omega C}. This means X_L < X_C. Now the Capacitor is the heavyweight champion! The capacitor dominates, so the circuit behaves like a capacitor. We call this a 'Capacitive' circuit. The current will lead the voltage.

Scenario 3: \omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}. This means X_L = X_C. The two opposite pulls are perfectly equal. They completely cancel each other out! It is a tie. With the inductor and capacitor effectively out of the picture, the only thing left acting on the current is the plain old Resistor. The circuit is purely 'Resistive'. This magical balance is what we call the 'Resonant Frequency'. 

By understanding who is 'pulling harder,' you can instantly tell the nature of any AC circuit!
questions.Answer: ωL > 1/ωC : Inductive | ωL = 1/ωC : Resistive | ωL < 1/ωC : Capacitive

questions.Concept: Reactance and Power in AC Circuits
questions.Question: 13. Statement I: The reactance of an AC circuit is zero. It is possible that the circuit contains a capacitor and an inductor. Statement II: In AC circuit, the average power delivered by the source never becomes zero. Choose the correct answer.
MCQ_Options: Statement I is true, Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false, Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
questions.Hint: For Statement I, think about resonance where components cancel out. For Statement II, think about what happens to power in a purely inductive or purely capacitive circuit.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's break down these two statements like a pro! Physics statements are like logic puzzles, and we have all the tools we need to solve them.

Let us test Statement I: 'The reactance of an AC circuit is zero. It is possible that the circuit contains a capacitor and an inductor.' Is this possible? Absolutely, YES! The total reactance of a series circuit is X = X_L - X_C. If we tune the frequency so that the inductive pushback perfectly matches the capacitive pushback (X_L = X_C), they subtract to exactly zero! This is the state of resonance. So, you can definitely have both components in the circuit and still have zero total reactance. Statement I is TRUE.

Let us test Statement II: 'In an AC circuit, the average power delivered by the source never becomes zero.' Is this true? Think about a circuit that ONLY has an inductor (or ONLY a capacitor). In these pure circuits, the voltage and current are exactly 90 degrees out of phase. For half the cycle, the source gives energy to the inductor to build its magnetic field. For the other half, the inductor collapses its field and gives the exact same energy BACK to the source! It is like passing a basketball back and forth—no energy is actually 'used up' or consumed over a full cycle. Therefore, the average power dissipated is strictly ZERO! Statement II claims power 'never' becomes zero, which is FALSE.

So, Statement I is true, but Statement II is terribly wrong!
questions.Answer: Statement I is true, Statement II is false

questions.Question: 14. To increase the resonant frequency in a series LCR circuit, what should be done?
MCQ_Options: Decrease the inductance (L) or decrease the capacitance (C)
Increase the inductance (L) or increase the capacitance (C)
questions.Hint: Look at the formula for resonant frequency: f_r = 1 / (2π√(LC)). How do you make the final answer (f_r) a bigger number?
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Remember the magic formula for resonant frequency? It is like tuning a guitar string to get the perfect pitch! The resonant frequency f_r of a series LCR circuit is the frequency where the inductor and capacitor perfectly balance each other out. 

The formula is: f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}. 

Let us look at this equation like a seesaw. The resonant frequency (f_r) is on the top left. The inductance (L) and the capacitance (C) are trapped inside a square root on the BOTTOM right (the denominator). 

In math, if you divide a pizza into fewer slices (making the denominator smaller), each slice gets bigger! So, if you want the frequency (f_r) to INCREASE, you must make the bottom of the fraction DECREASE. 

This means you need to either decrease the Inductance (L), or decrease the Capacitance (C), or do both! Notice that the Resistance (R) is completely missing from this formula. Changing the resistor will change how 'sharp' the resonance is (the Q-factor), but it will not shift the frequency left or right. 

So, to get a higher pitch (higher frequency), shrink your inductors and capacitors!
questions.Answer: Decrease the inductance (L) or decrease the capacitance (C)

questions.Question: 15. When you walk through a metal detector carrying a metal object, it raises an alarm. This phenomenon works on which principle?
questions.Hint: The metal detector creates a magnetic field. When metal enters this field, it changes the inductance of the coil inside the machine, knocking the circuit out of its tuned state.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Have you ever walked through a security scanner at an airport and heard it beep? It feels like magic, but it is pure physics at work, specifically relying on everything we just learned about Alternating Current!

The metal detector doorway is actually housing a giant coil of wire—an inductor! This inductor is connected to a capacitor, forming an LC circuit. The engineers tune this circuit so that it operates exactly at its resonant frequency. At resonance, as we learned, the current flowing through the circuit is at its absolute maximum peak.

Now, here is where the spy physics comes in. When you walk through the doorway carrying your keys or a metal belt buckle, that piece of metal enters the magnetic field of the inductor. The presence of this new metal instantly changes the magnetic properties of the space, which literally changes the Inductance (L) of the coil!

Look back at our formula: f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}. If L suddenly changes because of your keys, the resonant frequency of the doorway changes. But the machine is still pushing power at the OLD frequency! Because the machine is no longer perfectly matched to the circuit's new resonant frequency, the current drops dramatically. 

The computer inside the detector constantly measures this current. The second it sees the current drop from its maximum peak, it knows the inductance changed, which means METAL is present! It triggers the alarm—BEEP BEEP BEEP! Therefore, this amazing technology is based entirely on the principle of Resonance in AC circuits.

questions.Concept: Key Devices and Phenomena in AC
questions.Question: 16. Match List I with List II for AC generator, Transformer, Resonance phenomenon, Sharpness of resonance.
MCQ_Options: AC generator -> Electromagnetic Induction | Transformer -> Mutual Induction | Resonance -> X_L = X_C | Sharpness -> Q-factor
AC generator -> Mutual Induction | Transformer -> Electromagnetic Induction | Resonance -> Q-factor | Sharpness -> X_L = X_C
AC generator -> X_L = X_C | Transformer -> Q-factor | Resonance -> Mutual Induction | Sharpness -> Electromagnetic Induction
AC generator -> Q-factor | Transformer -> X_L = X_C | Resonance -> Electromagnetic Induction | Sharpness -> Mutual Induction
questions.Hint: Think about the fundamental principles behind each device. A generator creates voltage by spinning loops in a magnet. A transformer moves voltage between two stationary coils.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Here we have a magnificent summary of how electricity shapes our modern world. Let's match these grand concepts with their scientific principles!

1. AC Generator: How do power plants create the electricity that lights up your house? They use spinning coils of wire inside giant magnets! As the wire moves through the magnetic field, a voltage is created (induced) in the wire. This principle of turning motion and magnetism into electricity is called 'Electromagnetic Induction'.

2. Transformer: The black boxes on telephone poles are transformers. They change high voltages to safe, low voltages for your home. They do this by putting two coils of wire very close to each other. When AC current flows in the first coil, it creates a wiggling magnetic field, which forces a current to flow in the second coil! Because the first coil 'induces' a voltage in a mutual neighbor, this is called 'Mutual Induction'.

3. Resonance phenomenon: We have mastered this! Resonance occurs when the pushback from the inductor exactly equals the pushback from the capacitor, wiping each other out. This means Inductive Reactance equals Capacitive Reactance, or X_L = X_C.

4. Sharpness of resonance: When you tune a radio, you want to pick up one specific station clearly without hearing static from neighboring stations. A circuit with a very 'sharp' peak in its resonance curve is great at picking out just one frequency. The number that measures how sharp and precise this peak is is called the 'Quality factor' or 'Q-factor'. 

Matching these gives us the perfect overview of AC engineering!
questions.Answer: AC generator -> Electromagnetic Induction | Transformer -> Mutual Induction | Resonance -> X_L = X_C | Sharpness -> Q-factor

questions.Question: 17. Given below are two statements about maximum power dissipation in AC circuits. Choose the correct answer.
MCQ_Options: Maximum power dissipation occurs at resonance because the power factor is exactly 1
Maximum power dissipation occurs when purely inductive because the power factor is 0
Maximum power dissipation occurs when capacitive because voltage is high
Maximum power dissipation is constant across all frequencies
questions.Hint: Power is dissipated only by resistors. The formula for AC power includes a term called the power factor (cos φ). When is this factor at its maximum value of 1?
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Hello there! Even though we do not have the exact text of the two statements from the database, we can absolutely master the golden rule of power in AC circuits! This will allow you to answer ANY question they throw at you regarding maximum power.

In a DC circuit, Power is simply Voltage \times Current. But in an AC circuit, Voltage and Current are often out of sync (out of phase). If they aren't pushing together at the same time, the actual usable power drops! The formula for true power in AC is P = V_{rms} \times I_{rms} \times \cos(\phi), where \phi is the phase angle between voltage and current. 

That \cos(\phi) term is incredibly famous—it is called the 'Power Factor'. Its highest possible value is 1 (when \phi = 0 degrees). When does \phi equal 0? When the current and voltage are perfectly in sync! And when does THAT happen? When the circuit is purely resistive.

As we have learned, an LCR circuit acts purely resistive exactly at its resonant frequency (because the inductor and capacitor cancel each other out). At resonance, the impedance is at its lowest, so the current I_{rms} is at its maximum peak! 

So, at resonance, two amazing things happen simultaneously to maximize power: 
1) The current is as huge as it can possibly be. 
2) The power factor \cos(\phi) is exactly 1 (its absolute maximum). 
Therefore, maximum power dissipation in an AC circuit ALWAYS occurs at resonance. Remember this, and you will ace any power question!
questions.Answer: Maximum power dissipation occurs at resonance because the power factor is exactly 1

questions.Concept: Current vs Frequency Profile and Power Factor at Resonance
questions.Question: 18. Statement I: When frequency of AC source in series LCR circuit increases, current first increases, attains maximum, then decreases. Statement II: In series LCR circuit, power factor at resonance is one. Choose the correct answer.
MCQ_Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true, Statement II is false
Statement I is false, Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
questions.Hint: Draw a mental graph of current versus frequency for an LCR circuit. Does it form a mountain peak? Also, what is the phase difference between voltage and current at resonance?
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's wrap up our amazing AC physics journey by analyzing these two fantastic statements!

Statement I: 'When the frequency of an AC source in a series LCR circuit increases, current first increases, attains a maximum, then decreases.' 
Imagine starting at a very low frequency. The capacitor acts like a huge wall (high reactance), so very little current flows. As you slowly increase the frequency, the capacitor's wall shrinks, and current goes up! Eventually, you hit the magic Resonant Frequency, where total resistance (impedance) is incredibly low, and the current hits its absolute peak. But if you keep increasing the frequency past resonance, the Inductor starts acting like a new giant wall! So the current begins to drop back down. If you graph this, it looks exactly like a steep mountain peak. So, Statement I is a beautiful, completely TRUE description of reality.

Statement II: 'In a series LCR circuit, the power factor at resonance is one.' 
We just proved this in the last question! At resonance, the inductor and capacitor cancel out (X_L = X_C). The circuit acts as if it only has a simple resistor. In a purely resistive circuit, voltage and current hold hands and walk together perfectly in phase (phase angle \phi = 0\circ). The power factor is defined as \cos(\phi). Since \cos(0\circ) is exactly 1, the power factor is strictly 1. This means all the energy pushed by the source is actually doing real work! Statement II is completely TRUE.

Both statements are brilliantly correct. You have officially mastered AC circuit logic. Great job!
questions.Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are true

questions.Concept: Apparent Depth in Multiple Immiscible Liquids
questions.Question: 1. A vessel of depth 2h is half filled with liquid of refractive index 2√2 and upper half with liquid of refractive index √2. The liquids are immiscible. Find the apparent depth of the inner surface of the bottom.
questions.Hint: Treat each layer of liquid separately. The total apparent depth is just the sum of the apparent depths of each individual layer!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Hello there! Let's dive into this cool illusion. Have you ever looked into a swimming pool and thought the bottom looked closer than it actually is? That's because of refraction—light bending as it moves from water to air. The 'fake' depth you see is called the **Apparent Depth**.

Now, imagine instead of one liquid, we have two layers of different liquids stacked like a layer cake. The total depth of the vessel is $2h$, and it's half-filled with each liquid. So, each layer has an actual thickness of exactly $h$.

**Step 1: Understand the formula for apparent depth.**
For a single liquid of real depth $d$ and refractive index $\mu$, the apparent depth is simple: 
Apparent Depth = $\frac{\text{Real Depth}}{\mu}$.
Because the liquids are immiscible (they don't mix), we can just calculate the apparent depth for each layer and add them up! It's like finding the height of two stacked boxes.

**Step 2: Calculate for the bottom layer.**
The real depth is $h$. The refractive index is $\mu_1 = 2\sqrt{2}$.
Apparent depth of this layer = $\frac{h}{2\sqrt{2}}$.

**Step 3: Calculate for the top layer.**
The real depth is $h$. The refractive index is $\mu_2 = \sqrt{2}$.
Apparent depth of this layer = $\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**Step 4: Add them together!**
Total Apparent Depth = $\frac{h}{2\sqrt{2}} + \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}$.
To add these fractions, let's get a common denominator, which is $2\sqrt{2}$. 
We multiply the top and bottom of the second fraction by 2:
$\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2h}{2\sqrt{2}}$.
Now add: $\frac{h}{2\sqrt{2}} + \frac{2h}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3h}{2\sqrt{2}}$.

And there you have it! The bottom surface appears to be at a depth of $\frac{3h}{2\sqrt{2}}$. Physics is just building blocks, piece by piece!

questions.Concept: Total Internal Reflection and Snell's Window
questions.Question: 2. There is a small source of light at some depth below water surface (refractive index 4/3) in a large tank. Neglecting reflection and absorption, find the percentage of light that emerges out. Use surface area of spherical cap = 2πrh.
questions.Hint: Light only escapes through a cone defined by the critical angle (Snell's window). Use the solid angle formula to find what fraction of the total spherical light escapes!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Hey! This is a fascinating problem. Imagine you are a little glowing fish deep underwater. You shoot light rays in every single direction (like a perfect sphere). Does all your light escape the water? 

Nope! Because water is denser than air, light rays that hit the surface at a very sharp angle get trapped inside. This is called **Total Internal Reflection**. The light can only escape through a specific circular area on the surface right above you, which we call **Snell's Window**. It looks like a cone of light extending from you to the surface.

**Step 1: Find the Critical Angle.**
The edge of this cone is defined by the critical angle, $\theta_c$. The formula is:
$\sin(\theta_c) = \frac{1}{\mu}$
Since water has $\mu = 4/3$, we get $\sin(\theta_c) = \frac{3}{4}$. 
From this, we can find $\cos(\theta_c)$ using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta$:
$\cos(\theta_c) = \sqrt{1 - (\frac{3}{4})^2} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{9}{16}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{16}} = \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$.

**Step 2: Understand the 3D Geometry (Solid Angle).**
A full sphere of light has a 'solid angle' of $4\pi$ steradians. But the light only escapes through the cap of the cone. The solid angle $\Omega$ of a cone with half-angle $\theta_c$ is given by a super useful physics trick:
$\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta_c)$.

**Step 3: Calculate the fraction of light.**
The fraction of light that escapes is simply the solid angle of our cone divided by the solid angle of the whole sphere:
Fraction = $\frac{\Omega}{4\pi} = \frac{2\pi(1 - \cos\theta_c)}{4\pi} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta_c}{2}$.
Let's plug in $\cos(\theta_c)$:
Fraction = $\frac{1 - \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}}{2} = \frac{\frac{4 - \sqrt{7}}{4}}{2} = \frac{4 - \sqrt{7}}{8}$.

**Step 4: Convert to percentage.**
Percentage = Fraction $\times 100\%$.
$= \frac{4 - \sqrt{7}}{8} \times 100\% = 12.5(4 - \sqrt{7})\%$.
If you plug in the numbers ($\sqrt{7} \approx 2.64$), you get roughly $17\%$. Only $17\%$ of the light escapes! The rest bounces back into the depths.

questions.Question: 3. A point object is placed at 1 m in front of a convex lens of focal length 0.5 m. A plane mirror is placed at 2 m behind the lens. Find the position and nature of the final image.
MCQ_Options: 0.6 m in front of the lens, Real
questions.Hint: Follow the light ray! It goes through the lens, hits the mirror, and then reflects BACK through the lens again. Apply the lens formula, then mirror, then lens again.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Alright, let's track a beam of light on a wild journey! When dealing with multiple optical instruments, just take it one step at a time. The image formed by the first instrument becomes the object for the second instrument.

**Journey Part 1: Through the Lens.**
The light leaves the object and hits the convex lens. 
Object distance, $u = -1$ m (we use negative because it's against the direction of light).
Focal length, $f = +0.5$ m (convex lenses have positive focal lengths).
Let's use the Lens Formula: $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$.
$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{-1} = \frac{1}{0.5}$
$\frac{1}{v} + 1 = 2$
$\frac{1}{v} = 1 \implies v = 1$ m.
So, the lens forms a real image 1 meter behind the lens.

**Journey Part 2: Bouncing off the Plane Mirror.**
Our plane mirror is waiting 2 meters behind the lens. 
The lens just formed an image 1 meter behind the lens, which means this image is 1 meter IN FRONT of the mirror (because 2m - 1m = 1m). 
Plane mirrors are simple: they form an image as far behind the mirror as the object is in front. So, the mirror forms a virtual image 1 meter BEHIND the mirror.
Now, how far is this new image from our original lens? It's 2 meters (distance to mirror) + 1 meter (behind mirror) = 3 meters behind the lens.

**Journey Part 3: Back through the Lens.**
The light reflected off the mirror travels backward and hits the lens again. 
To the lens, the light appears to be coming from the mirror's image, which is 3 meters away.
Since light is traveling backward now, we flip our sign convention. The object distance $u' = -3$ m. 
Focal length $f = +0.5$ m.
Let's use the Lens Formula again:
$\frac{1}{v'} - \frac{1}{-3} = \frac{1}{0.5}$
$\frac{1}{v'} + \frac{1}{3} = 2$
$\frac{1}{v'} = 2 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{3}$
$v' = \frac{3}{5}$ m = 0.6 m.
Since $v'$ is positive, the final image is formed 0.6 m in the direction the light is traveling (which is 'in front' of the lens relative to the original setup). Since the rays physically intersect there, it is a Real image.
Awesome job! You just traced light through three events!
questions.Answer: 0.6 m in front of the lens, Real

questions.Question: 4. An observer can see through a small hole on the side of a jar (radius 15 cm) at height 15 cm from bottom. The hole is at height 45 cm. When the jar is filled with liquid up to height 30 cm, the observer can see the edge at the bottom. If refractive index of liquid is N/100, find N.
questions.Hint: Draw two triangles! The first path (empty jar) tells you the observer's viewing angle. The second path (filled jar) bends the light from the bottom edge to that exact same angle. Use Snell's Law!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's become a detective to solve this one. We need to visualize the geometry of what the observer can see before and after pouring the liquid.

**Clue 1: The Empty Jar.**
The jar has a radius of $15$ cm, which means its full width (diameter) is $30$ cm. 
The observer looks through a hole at a height of $45$ cm on one side of the jar. When the jar is empty, their line of sight hits the opposite wall at a height of $15$ cm from the bottom.
Let's form a triangle! The horizontal distance across the jar is $30$ cm. The vertical drop from the hole ($45$ cm) to the target ($15$ cm) is $45 - 15 = 30$ cm. 
Aha! The vertical drop and horizontal distance are both $30$ cm. This creates a neat right-angled triangle with equal sides. This means the observer's line of sight makes an angle of $45^\circ$ with the vertical.

**Clue 2: The Filled Jar.**
Now, fill the jar with liquid up to $30$ cm. The observer, looking down exactly the same $45^\circ$ path, can now magically see the bottom edge of the jar (which is horizontally $30$ cm away on the bottom floor, coordinate $0,0$ if the hole is at $30,45$).
Why? Because the liquid bends (refracts) the light! 
Let's trace the light from the bottom edge to the observer. 
The light travels from the bottom edge $(0, 0)$ up to the liquid surface at height $30$ cm. Where does it exit the liquid? 
Since the observer's viewing angle is fixed at $45^\circ$, the ray from the liquid surface up to the hole must be at a $45^\circ$ angle. 
The distance from the surface ($30$ cm high) to the hole ($45$ cm high) is $15$ cm vertically. With a $45^\circ$ angle, the horizontal distance must also be $15$ cm. 
So, the ray exits the liquid right in the middle of the jar (horizontal distance $15$ cm).

**Clue 3: Inside the Liquid.**
The light traveled from the edge $(0,0)$ to the exit point $(15, 30)$. 
Let's find the angle of incidence ($i$) inside the liquid. The horizontal change is $15$, vertical change is $30$. 
$\tan(i) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.5$.
Using a bit of triangle math, if $\tan(i) = \frac{1}{2}$, then the hypotenuse is $\sqrt{1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5}$. 
So, $\sin(i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$.

**Step 4: Snell's Law!**
$\mu \times \sin(i) = 1 \times \sin(r)$.
The angle of refraction $r$ is $45^\circ$ (the observer's angle).
$\mu \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} = \sin(45^\circ) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
$\mu = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581$.

The question asks for $N/100 = 1.581$, which means $N = 158.1$. 
Rounding to the nearest integer, $N = 158$. Great job visualizing!

questions.Concept: Refraction and Reflection in Spherical Media
questions.Question: 5. A light ray enters a solid glass sphere of refractive index μ = √3 at angle of incidence 60°. The ray is both reflected and refracted at the farther surface. Find the angle between the reflected and refracted rays.
questions.Hint: Draw a circle and map the angles! The triangle formed by the center of the sphere and the light ray inside is an isosceles triangle (two sides are the radius).
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Imagine a shiny glass crystal ball. A light ray strikes it, enters, travels across the inside, and hits the back wall. At that back wall, it splits! Some light breaks out (refraction) and some bounces back in (reflection). We need to find the angle between these two splitting beams.

**Step 1: The First Refraction (Entering the sphere).**
The ray hits the outside at an angle of incidence $i = 60^\circ$. 
Let's use Snell's Law to find the angle it bends inside ($r_1$):
$1 \times \sin(60^\circ) = \mu \times \sin(r_1)$
$1 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sqrt{3} \times \sin(r_1)$
$\sin(r_1) = \frac{1}{2} \implies r_1 = 30^\circ$.
So, the ray bends to $30^\circ$ inside the glass.

**Step 2: Traveling to the back wall.**
Draw a straight line for the ray inside the circle. Now draw two lines from the center of the sphere to where the ray enters and exits. These two lines are both radii of the sphere, so they are equal! 
This forms an isosceles triangle. Because the angles opposite to equal sides are equal, the angle of incidence at the back wall ($i_2$) is exactly the same as the angle it entered with: $i_2 = 30^\circ$.

**Step 3: The Split at the Back Wall.**
Event A: **Reflection**. The law of reflection says angle of incidence = angle of reflection. So the reflected ray bounces back inside at $30^\circ$ from the normal.
Event B: **Refraction**. Let's use Snell's law to see how the light bends as it escapes into the air:
$\mu \times \sin(30^\circ) = 1 \times \sin(r_2)$
$\sqrt{3} \times \frac{1}{2} = \sin(r_2) \implies \sin(r_2) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \implies r_2 = 60^\circ$.
The escaping ray bends $60^\circ$ from the normal.

**Step 4: Find the angle between them.**
The normal is a straight line. The reflected ray is on one side of the normal (inside) at $30^\circ$. The refracted ray is on the other side (outside) at $60^\circ$. 
Wait, let's visualize the straight normal line ($180^\circ$). 
The angle between the surface normal pointing inward and the reflected ray is $30^\circ$.
The angle between the surface normal pointing outward and the refracted ray is $60^\circ$.
Because they are on opposite sides of the boundary but measured from the same normal line going straight through, the angle between the two rays is $180^\circ - (30^\circ + 60^\circ) = 90^\circ$.
They are perfectly perpendicular! That's a beautiful, neat physics trick.

questions.Concept: Field of View of a Plane Mirror
questions.Question: 6. A point source of light S is placed at 60 cm in front of the centre of a plane mirror of width 50 cm hanging vertically. A man walks in front of the mirror along a line parallel to the mirror at distance 1.2 m from it. Find the distance between the extreme points where he can see the image.
questions.Hint: Treat the virtual image behind the mirror as a real lightbulb shining through a 'window' (the mirror). Use similar triangles to find how wide the light spreads out at the man's walking path.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
This problem is all about 'Field of View'. Think of looking through a window into a room. The farther back you stand from the window, the less of the room you see. A mirror is exactly like a window, but instead of looking at a real room, you are looking at a virtual 'mirror room'.

**Step 1: Locate the Virtual Image.**
The light source $S$ is 60 cm ($0.6$ m) in front of the mirror. 
Plane mirrors act like perfect clones. The image $S'$ is formed exactly $0.6$ m BEHIND the mirror.

**Step 2: Set up the Geometry.**
Imagine the image $S'$ is a real light bulb shining light through a window (the mirror) that is $50$ cm ($0.5$ m) wide. 
The light rays from $S'$ will pass through the two extreme edges of the mirror and keep traveling outward, creating a wider and wider cone of visible area.
The man walks on a line that is $1.2$ m in front of the mirror. 
So, how far is the man's path from the virtual bulb $S'$? 
Distance = $0.6$ m (from $S'$ to mirror) + $1.2$ m (from mirror to man) = $1.8$ m.

**Step 3: Similar Triangles!**
We have a small triangle and a big triangle.
Small Triangle: From the virtual bulb $S'$ to the edges of the mirror. Its height is $0.6$ m and its base (width of mirror) is $0.5$ m.
Big Triangle: From the virtual bulb $S'$ all the way to the man's path. Its height is $1.8$ m. Let's call its base (the width of the field of view) $W$.
Because light travels in straight lines, these triangles are proportional (similar).
$\frac{\text{Width of Field of View}}{\text{Total Height}} = \frac{\text{Width of Mirror}}{\text{Distance to Mirror}}$
$\frac{W}{1.8} = \frac{0.5}{0.6}$
$W = 1.8 \times (\frac{0.5}{0.6}) = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5$ meters.

Convert that to centimeters: $1.5 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ cm}$.
The man has a $150$ cm wide zone where he can peek into the mirror and see the light. Once he steps out of that zone, the mirror 'window' cuts off his view!

questions.Concept: Magnification and Types of Spherical Mirrors
questions.Question: 7. A short straight object of height 100 cm lies before the central axis of a spherical mirror with |f| = 40 cm. The image is of height 25 cm with same orientation. What can be concluded about the mirror and image?
questions.Hint: Look at the height and orientation! If the image is upright ('same orientation') and smaller than the object, there is only one type of mirror that can do that.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's put on our detective hats and figure out the identity of this mystery mirror! We have three big clues.

**Clue 1: Same Orientation (Upright).**
The problem states the image has the 'same orientation' as the object. In optics, an upright image is ALWAYS a **Virtual** image. Real images (formed by actual converging light rays) always flip upside down. So we already know the image is Virtual! 

**Clue 2: Diminished (Smaller).**
The object is huge: $100$ cm tall. The image is small: $25$ cm tall. 
The magnification $m$ = $\frac{\text{Height of Image}}{\text{Height of Object}}$ = $\frac{25}{100} = +0.25$. (It's positive because it's upright).

**Clue 3: Which mirror does this?**
Let's review our mirror types:
1. **Plane Mirrors:** Always make virtual, upright images, but they are exactly the SAME size ($m=1$). 
2. **Concave Mirrors:** Can make virtual, upright images, but they act like magnifying makeup mirrors—the virtual images are always BIGGER than the object ($m > 1$).
3. **Convex Mirrors:** These are the security mirrors you see in convenience stores or on car side mirrors. They always produce virtual, upright, and SMALLER images ($0 < m < 1$). 

Since our magnification is $+0.25$ (smaller and upright), this MUST be a **Convex Mirror**! 

We can even verify with math using $m = \frac{f}{f - u}$. 
$0.25 = \frac{40}{40 - u}$. 
$10 - 0.25u = 40 \implies -0.25u = 30 \implies u = -120$ cm. 
The math perfectly checks out. It's a Convex mirror making a Virtual image.

questions.Question: 8. A deviation of 2° is produced in yellow ray when crown and flint glass prisms are achromatically combined. Dispersive powers are 0.02 and 0.03, refractive indices for yellow are 1.5 and 1.6. Find the refracting angle of crown glass prism.
questions.Hint: Achromatic combination means the 'rainbow' (dispersion) cancels out perfectly, but the bending (deviation) doesn't! Set the dispersions equal to each other to find the ratio of deviations.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Have you ever played with a prism and seen white light split into a beautiful rainbow? That spreading out is called **dispersion**. The bending of the light is called **deviation**.
Sometimes, scientists want to bend light (deviate it) WITHOUT splitting it into a rainbow (no dispersion). To do this, they glue two different prisms together upside-down to each other. One prism spreads the colors, and the second one puts them back together into white light! This is called an **Achromatic Combination**.

**Step 1: The Zero Dispersion Rule.**
For the rainbow to cancel out completely, the dispersion of the Crown glass must equal the dispersion of the Flint glass. 
Dispersion = Dispersive Power ($\omega$) $\times$ Deviation ($\delta$).
So, $\omega_C \times \delta_C = \omega_F \times \delta_F$.
Let's plug in the given powers:
$0.02 \times \delta_C = 0.03 \times \delta_F$.
This means $\delta_F = \frac{2}{3} \delta_C$.

**Step 2: The Net Deviation.**
Even though the rainbow is canceled out, the light still gets bent a little overall. Because the prisms are opposing each other, their deviations subtract.
Net Deviation = $\delta_C - \delta_F$ (assuming crown bends it more).
We know the Net Deviation is $2^\circ$.
$2^\circ = \delta_C - \frac{2}{3}\delta_C$
$2^\circ = \frac{1}{3} \delta_C$
Multiply both sides by 3:
$\delta_C = 6^\circ$.
The crown glass bends the light by $6^\circ$!

**Step 3: Find the Prism Angle.**
The formula for the deviation of a thin prism is: $\delta = (\mu - 1) \times A$, where $A$ is the refracting angle of the prism.
For the crown glass:
$6^\circ = (1.5 - 1) \times A_C$
$6^\circ = 0.5 \times A_C$
$A_C = \frac{6^\circ}{0.5} = 12^\circ$.
The refracting angle of the crown glass prism is $12^\circ$. Awesome optical engineering!

questions.Question: 9. The thickness at the centre of a plano convex lens is 3 mm and diameter is 6 cm. Speed of light in the lens material is 2 × 10⁸ m/s. Find the focal length.
questions.Hint: First use the speed of light to find the refractive index. Then, use the geometry of a circle (Sagitta theorem) with thickness and diameter to find the Radius of Curvature. Finally, use the Lens Maker's Formula!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's build a lens from scratch! To find its focal length, we need to know what it's made of (refractive index) and its shape (radius of curvature).

**Step 1: Find the Refractive Index ($\mu$).**
Refractive index is just the 'speed limit' of light in a material compared to space.
$\mu = \frac{\text{Speed of light in vacuum (c)}}{\text{Speed of light in material (v)}}$
$\mu = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 1.5$.
Our glass has $\mu = 1.5$.

**Step 2: Find the Radius of Curvature (R).**
A plano-convex lens is flat on one side and curved on the other. Think of the curved side as a slice chopped off from a larger sphere. 
We know the 'width' (diameter) of the slice is $6$ cm, so its radius $r = 3$ cm. The thickness at the center $t = 3$ mm $= 0.3$ cm.
There is a cool geometry trick called the Intersecting Chords Theorem (or Sagitta formula) relating these: 
$R \approx \frac{r^2}{2t}$ (This approximation is extremely accurate when thickness is very small compared to width, which is true for thin lenses).
Let's calculate $R$:
$R = \frac{3^2}{2 \times 0.3} = \frac{9}{0.6} = 15$ cm.
So the curved surface belongs to a sphere with a radius of $15$ cm.

**Step 3: Lens Maker's Formula.**
Now we plug our ingredients into the master formula:
$\frac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1) \times (\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2})$
For a plano-convex lens, one surface is curved ($R_1 = 15$ cm) and the other is perfectly flat ($R_2 = \infty$). 
$\frac{1}{\infty}$ is exactly 0!
$\frac{1}{f} = (1.5 - 1) \times (\frac{1}{15} - 0)$
$\frac{1}{f} = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{15} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{15} = \frac{1}{30}$.
Flip it over, and we get $f = 30$ cm.
The lens will focus light perfectly at 30 cm away!

questions.Concept: Refraction at a Single Spherical Surface
questions.Question: 10. The image of an object placed in air formed by a convex refracting surface is at 10 m behind the surface. The image is real and at 2/3 of the object distance. Wavelength inside the surface is 2/3 times wavelength in air. Radius of curved surface is x/13 m. Find x.
questions.Hint: Wavelength change tells you the refractive index. Use the single spherical surface refraction formula: (μ2/v) - (μ1/u) = (μ2 - μ1)/R.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Let's decode this step-by-step. Imagine a solid glass dome. Light hits the curved surface and bends inside to form an image. We need to find the curve's radius.

**Step 1: Unlocking the Refractive Index ($\mu$).**
When light enters a denser material, it slows down. The frequency stays the same, but the waves get squished (wavelength decreases). 
The formula is: $\mu = \frac{\text{Wavelength in air}}{\text{Wavelength in material}}$.
The problem says wavelength inside is $\frac{2}{3}$ of the air. So, $\mu = \frac{1}{2/3} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$.
The material has a refractive index of $1.5$.

**Step 2: Object and Image Distances.**
We are told the image is REAL and forms 10 m behind the surface. 
Following sign conventions (light travels left to right):
Image distance $v = +10$ m.
We are also told the image distance is $\frac{2}{3}$ of the object distance. 
$10 = \frac{2}{3} \times |u| \implies |u| = 15$ m.
Since the object is in front of the surface, $u = -15$ m.

**Step 3: The Spherical Surface Formula.**
There is a special formula for light bending just once at a curved surface:
$\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R}$.
Here, $\mu_1 = 1$ (air) and $\mu_2 = 1.5$ (material).
$\frac{1.5}{10} - \frac{1}{-15} = \frac{1.5 - 1}{R}$.
Let's make the math easier by using fractions ($1.5 = 3/2$):
$\frac{3/2}{10} + \frac{1}{15} = \frac{0.5}{R}$.
$\frac{3}{20} + \frac{1}{15} = \frac{1}{2R}$.
Find a common denominator for 20 and 15, which is 60:
$\frac{9}{60} + \frac{4}{60} = \frac{1}{2R}$.
$\frac{13}{60} = \frac{1}{2R}$.
Cross multiply!
$13 \times 2R = 60 \times 1 \implies 26R = 60 \implies R = \frac{60}{26} = \frac{30}{13}$ meters.

The problem states the radius is $\frac{x}{13}$ m. Comparing this to our answer $\frac{30}{13}$, it's clear that $x = 30$! Piece of cake.

questions.Concept: Total Internal Reflection in a Slab/Fiber
questions.Question: 11. A ray of light enters from air into a denser medium of refractive index 4/3. It suffers total internal reflection at the adjacent surface. Find the maximum value of angle θ.
questions.Hint: Use Snell's Law at the first face. Then use geometry to find the angle of incidence at the adjacent (side) wall. For it to reflect totally, that side angle must be greater than the critical angle!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Imagine shining a laser pointer into the flat end of a glass rectangle (like an optical fiber). You want the light to bounce off the 'walls' inside without escaping. 

**Step 1: The Entry Point.**
Let the laser enter the flat end at an angle $\theta$ from the air ($\mu = 1$). It bends inside the material ($\mu = 4/3$) at an angle $r$.
By Snell's Law: $1 \times \sin(\theta) = \frac{4}{3} \sin(r)$. 

**Step 2: Hitting the Wall.**
The ray travels straight until it hits the side wall (adjacent surface). Because it entered a rectangle, the angle it hits the side wall (let's call it $i'$) is complementary to $r$. 
Think of a right triangle inside the corner. $r + i' = 90^\circ$, which means $i' = 90^\circ - r$.

**Step 3: Total Internal Reflection (TIR).**
For the light to bounce off the wall like a perfect mirror (TIR) and not escape, the angle it hits the wall ($i'$) must be equal to or greater than the critical angle $\theta_c$.
The rule for critical angle is: $\sin(\theta_c) = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{4/3} = \frac{3}{4}$.
So, we need $\sin(i') \ge \frac{3}{4}$.
Since $i' = 90^\circ - r$, we know $\sin(i') = \sin(90^\circ - r) = \cos(r)$.
Therefore, we need $\cos(r) \ge \frac{3}{4}$.

**Step 4: Maximizing $\theta$.**
The question asks for the MAXIMUM entry angle $\theta$. If you make $\theta$ bigger, the inside angle $r$ gets bigger too. But if $r$ gets bigger, $\cos(r)$ gets smaller! 
To keep $\cos(r)$ from dropping below $3/4$, the absolute maximum we can push it is exactly when $\cos(r) = \frac{3}{4}$.
If $\cos(r) = \frac{3}{4}$, then by Pythagorean theorem, $\sin(r) = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(r)} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{9}{16}} = \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$.

**Step 5: Final calculation.**
Now, take this $\sin(r)$ back to our very first Snell's Law equation:
$\sin(\theta) = \frac{4}{3} \times \sin(r) = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$.
The 4s cancel out perfectly!
$\sin(\theta) = \frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}$.
So, the maximum angle $\theta = \arcsin(\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3})$. That's how we trap light perfectly!

questions.Question: 12. A prism of refractive index n₁ and another of n₂ are stuck together. n₁ = 1.2 + (10.8 × 10^{-14})/λ², n₂ = 1.45 + (1.8 × 10^{-14})/λ². Find the wavelength for which rays incident at any angle on interface BC pass through without bending.
questions.Hint: If a light ray passes from one medium to another without bending regardless of the angle, both media must appear optically identical to that light ray. Set the two equations equal to each other!
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Imagine you are running on a paved road, and suddenly the road turns into thick, sticky mud. Because the mud slows you down, your path might bend or swerve as you cross the boundary. In physics, this 'bending' of light is called refraction, and it happens because light travels at different speeds in different materials. The 'refractive index' (denoted by n) is just a measure of how 'muddy' or optically dense a material is. 

Now, the problem tells us a fascinating secret: for one specific color (wavelength, λ) of light, the ray passes through the boundary between the two prisms without bending at all! What does this mean conceptually? It means that to this specific light ray, both prisms feel exactly the same. The optical 'muddiness' is identical. In mathematical terms, the refractive index of the first prism must equal the refractive index of the second prism (n₁ = n₂). 

Let's set up our equation based on this brilliant realization:
1.2 +  rac{10.8 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2} = 1.45 +  rac{1.8 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2}

Now, let's play with the algebra to solve for our mystery wavelength (λ). We want to get all the terms with λ on one side and all the plain numbers on the other side.
Subtract 1.2 from both sides:
 rac{10.8 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2} = 0.25 +  rac{1.8 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2}

Next, subtract the smaller fraction from both sides:
 rac{10.8 	imes 10^{-14} - 1.8 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2} = 0.25

Simplify the top:
 rac{9.0 	imes 10^{-14}}{\lambda^2} = 0.25

Now, we rearrange to solve for λ²:
\lambda^2 =  rac{9.0 	imes 10^{-14}}{0.25}

Dividing by 0.25 is exactly the same as multiplying by 4, right? So:
\lambda^2 = 9.0 	imes 10^{-14} 	imes 4 = 36 	imes 10^{-14} m²

Finally, we take the square root of both sides to find λ:
\lambda = \sqrt{36 	imes 10^{-14}} = 6 	imes 10^{-7} meters.

To make this number prettier, we can convert it into nanometers (nm), which is how we usually measure light. Since 1 meter is 10^9 nanometers, we multiply by 10^9:
6 	imes 10^{-7} 	imes 10^9 = 600 nm.

This means that orange light (which has a wavelength of around 600 nm) will pass straight through the boundary like it isn't even there! You just used algebra to unlock a hidden property of light.

questions.Concept: Velocity of Image in Spherical Mirrors
questions.Question: 13. Car B overtakes car A at relative speed 40 m/s. How fast will the image of car B appear to move in the mirror of focal length 10 cm fitted in car A, when car B is 1.9 m away?
questions.Hint: The speed of an image in a mirror isn't the same as the object! The formula relating object velocity and image velocity along the central axis is v_image = m² × v_object, where m is the magnification.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Have you ever looked at the side mirror of a car and seen the warning: 'Objects in mirror are closer than they appear'? That happens because car side mirrors are convex mirrors. They shrink the images of the cars behind you so you can see a wider field of view. But here is something even crazier: because they shrink the size of the car, they also shrink the SPEED at which the car appears to be moving! Let's figure out exactly how much the speed shrinks.

First, let's list what we know:
1. The mirror is a rear-view mirror fitted to a car, meaning it's a convex mirror. Convex mirrors have a positive focal length, so f = +10 cm = +0.1 m.
2. Car B is behind Car A at a distance of 1.9 m. In optics, we use a sign convention where distances in front of the mirror are negative. So, the object distance u = -1.9 m.
3. The relative speed at which Car B is approaching the mirror is 40 m/s. So, v_object = 40 m/s.

To find out how fast the image moves, we first need to find the 'magnification' (m) of the mirror at this exact moment. Magnification tells us how much smaller the image is compared to the real object. We can find it using the formula:
m =  rac{f}{f - u}

Let's plug in our numbers:
m =  rac{0.1}{0.1 - (-1.9)} =  rac{0.1}{0.1 + 1.9} =  rac{0.1}{2.0} =  rac{1}{20}.

This means the image of the car is 20 times smaller than the real car! Now, here comes the beautiful physics trick. If an image is squished by a factor of 20 in height, it is also squished by a factor of 20 in its length (depth). And since speed is just distance over time, the image speed is squished by that factor SQUARED. Why squared? Because as the object moves, the magnification itself changes slightly, compounding the squish! The formula for image speed along the axis is:
v_{image} = m^2 	imes v_{object}

Let's calculate the image speed:
v_{image} = ( rac{1}{20})^2 	imes 40 m/s
v_{image} =  rac{1}{400} 	imes 40 m/s
v_{image} =  rac{40}{400} m/s = 0.1 m/s.

So, even though Car B is roaring past at a terrifying 40 m/s, its tiny reflection in the mirror is gently strolling across the glass at just 0.1 m/s (or 10 cm per second). Isn't it amazing how math predicts exactly what we see in the real world?

questions.Question: 14. An object is placed at 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on the other side of lens at 8 cm. Image coincides with the object. When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed. Find the distance of this image from the object.
questions.Hint: If the final image perfectly coincides with the object, the light rays must have retraced their exact paths! This only happens if they hit the convex mirror at exactly a 90-degree angle, meaning they were heading straight toward the mirror's center of curvature.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
This problem feels like a magic trick! We have a light ray that goes out from an object, travels through a lens, hits a mirror, and bounces all the way back to exactly where it started. In physics, we call this 'autocollimation' or the principle of reversibility. 

Let's break down how this magic trick works. Imagine throwing a tennis ball at a wall. If you want the ball to bounce back and hit you perfectly in the hand, you have to throw it at exactly a 90-degree angle (perpendicular) to the wall. Light behaves the same way! For the light rays to bounce back and form an image precisely on top of the original object, they MUST have hit the convex mirror perfectly perpendicular to its curved surface.

But what does 'perpendicular to a curved surface' mean? For a sphere (like a spherical mirror), any line drawn toward its dead-center (the Center of Curvature) hits the surface at exactly 90 degrees. So, the lens must have been trying to project an image exactly at the mirror's Center of Curvature! 

Let's find out where that Center of Curvature is. The convex mirror has a focal length (f) of 15 cm. The radius of curvature (R) is simply twice the focal length:
R = 2 	imes 15 cm = 30 cm.
So, the lens was trying to form an image 30 cm behind the mirror. 

Now, where is this spot relative to the lens? The problem states the mirror is 8 cm away from the lens. Therefore, the total distance from the lens to this planned image spot is:
Image distance from lens (v) = 8 cm (distance to mirror) + 30 cm (distance from mirror to center) = 38 cm.

Now, read the second part of the question: 'When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed.' 
If we take the mirror away, the light rays aren't interrupted anymore. They will just keep traveling until they form the image exactly where they were aiming! So, the real, inverted image will be formed at v = 38 cm on the other side of the lens.

Finally, the question asks for the distance of this image from the OBJECT. 
We know the object is 12 cm in front of the lens. The image is 38 cm behind the lens. To find the total distance between the object and the image, we just add these two distances together:
Total Distance = 12 cm + 38 cm = 50 cm.

You've successfully dismantled an optical illusion. The image forms 50 cm away from the original object!

questions.Question: 15. Cross-section view of a prism is equilateral triangle ABC. Minimum deviation is observed when angle of incidence equals prism angle. Find the time taken by light to travel from P (midpoint of BC) to A.
questions.Hint: First, use the condition of minimum deviation to find the refractive index of the prism. Then, remember that time = distance / speed. The speed of light inside the prism is c/μ. (Assume the side length of the equilateral triangle is L).
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Imagine a triangular prism as a tiny mountain of glass. Light climbs up one side, travels through the middle, and goes down the other side. The problem tells us two very important things: the prism is an equilateral triangle, and the light is undergoing 'minimum deviation.'

Let's decode this step-by-step!

Step 1: The Prism Geometry
Because it's an equilateral triangle, all its angles are 60°. So, the 'prism angle' (A) is 60°. The text doesn't give us the side length, which is a common quirk in advanced physics problems. When this happens, we assign a variable. Let's call the side length of the triangle 'L'.

Step 2: The Magic of Minimum Deviation
'Minimum deviation' is a special, perfectly symmetrical state. It means the light ray travels horizontally through the prism, parallel to the base. The problem states that during this state, the angle of incidence (i) equals the prism angle (A). 
So, i = A = 60°.
Because it's minimum deviation, the angle of refraction inside the glass (r) is exactly half the prism angle:
r = A / 2 = 60° / 2 = 30°.

Step 3: Finding the Refractive Index (μ)
Refractive index tells us how much the glass slows down light. We use Snell's Law to find it:
\mu =  rac{\sin(i)}{\sin(r)} =  rac{\sin(60°)}{\sin(30°)}
We know that sin(60°) =  rac{\sqrt{3}}{2} and sin(30°) =  rac{1}{2}. Let's plug them in:
\mu =  rac{ rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{ rac{1}{2}} = \sqrt{3}.
The refractive index of our prism is √3.

Step 4: Finding the Speed of Light Inside the Prism
Light is lazy—when it enters a thicker medium, it slows down. The new speed (v) is the speed of light in a vacuum (c) divided by the refractive index:
v =  rac{c}{\sqrt{3}}.

Step 5: Finding the Distance
The question asks for the time it takes light to travel straight from P (the midpoint of the base BC) to A (the very top tip of the triangle). This straight line is the height (altitude) of an equilateral triangle. Using basic geometry, the height (h) of an equilateral triangle with side L is:
h = L 	imes  rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.

Step 6: Calculating the Time
Time is just distance divided by speed.
Time =  rac{Distance}{Speed} =  rac{h}{v}
Time =  rac{L  rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{ rac{c}{\sqrt{3}}}
When you divide by a fraction, you flip it and multiply:
Time = (L 	imes  rac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) 	imes ( rac{\sqrt{3}}{c})
Multiply the square roots together (√3 × √3 = 3):
Time =  rac{3L}{2c}.

And there we have it! By combining Snell's Law, geometry, and basic kinematics, we found the time light takes to scale the inside of the glass mountain.

questions.Concept: Apparent Depth and Refraction at Plane Surfaces
questions.Question: 16. A glass tumbler of inner depth 17.5 cm is kept on a table. A student pours water (μ = 4/3) while looking from above. When he feels the tumbler is half filled, he stops. Find the actual height of water.
questions.Hint: Because of refraction, water looks shallower than it really is. If the tumbler 'feels' half full, it means the visible empty space at the top looks exactly equal to the 'apparent' depth of the water at the bottom.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Have you ever stood in a swimming pool, looked down at your feet, and noticed your legs look surprisingly short and stubby? That’s an optical illusion caused by refraction! When light travels from water into air, it bends, making the bottom of the pool—or in this case, a glass tumbler—seem much closer than it actually is. We call this 'Apparent Depth'.

Let’s visualize the student pouring water into the tumbler. 
The total depth of the tumbler is 17.5 cm. Let's say the student pours water up to a real, actual height of 'x' cm. 
This means the empty part of the tumbler (the air part on top) has a height of (17.5 - x) cm.

Now, the student is looking straight down from the top. He sees the empty part normally because it's just air. But the water part is playing tricks on his eyes! Because of refraction, the water looks shallower. 
The formula for Apparent Depth is:
Apparent Depth =  rac{Actual Depth}{\mu}
Here, the actual depth is x, and the refractive index of water (μ) is 4/3. So:
Apparent Depth =  rac{x}{ rac{4}{3}} =  rac{3x}{4}.

Now, let's look at the crucial clue: 'When he feels the tumbler is half filled, he stops.' 
What does it mean to 'feel' or 'look' half filled? It means the top half (the empty part) looks exactly equal in size to the bottom half (the water part). 
So, the height of the empty space equals the apparent height of the water!
Let's write this as an equation:
17.5 - x =  rac{3x}{4}

Now, we just solve for x, which is our real height! Let's get all the 'x' terms on one side by adding x to both sides:
17.5 = x +  rac{3x}{4}

To add these together, think of x as  rac{4x}{4}:
17.5 =  rac{4x}{4} +  rac{3x}{4}
17.5 =  rac{7x}{4}

Now, multiply both sides by 4 to get rid of the fraction:
17.5 	imes 4 = 7x
70 = 7x

Divide by 7:
x = 10 cm.

The student thought he filled it exactly halfway (which would be 8.75 cm), but because the water was deceiving his eyes by lifting the bottom up, he actually poured in 10 cm of water! Refraction is the ultimate prankster.

questions.Question: 17. The distance between object and its 3 times magnified virtual image produced by a convex lens is 20 cm. Find the focal length.
questions.Hint: A virtual image in a convex lens means the object and image are on the same side. Use the magnification formula (v = 3u) and the distance between them (|v| - |u| = 20) to find the object and image coordinates, then use the lens formula.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Think about how a magnifying glass works. When you hold it close to an object (like a bug or a book), you see a massive, upright version of it. Because the image is upright and you have to look through the lens to see it, we call it a 'virtual' image. Furthermore, for a virtual image created by a single lens, the image always forms on the SAME side of the lens as the object, but farther back.

Let's translate the text into physics coordinates. We always place the object on the left side of the lens, so the object distance (u) is negative. Let's call its coordinate 'u'.
Because the image is virtual and magnified 3 times, the magnification (m) is +3. The formula for lens magnification is:
m =  rac{v}{u} = 3 \implies v = 3u.
Since u is negative, v is also negative, which perfectly matches our logic that the image is on the same side, just 3 times farther from the lens!

The problem tells us the physical distance between the object and the image is 20 cm. Imagine the lens is at position 0. The object is at some negative mark, and the image is at an even bigger negative mark behind it. The gap between them is 20 cm.
Mathematically:
|v| - |u| = 20
Since |v| is just 3 times |u|:
3|u| - |u| = 20
2|u| = 20
|u| = 10 cm.

Awesome! Now we have our precise coordinates. The object is 10 cm in front of the lens, and the image is 30 cm in front of the lens.
So, u = -10 cm, and v = -30 cm.

Now we have everything we need to find the focal length (f) using the thin lens formula:
 rac{1}{f} =  rac{1}{v} -  rac{1}{u}

Let's plug in our numbers safely:
 rac{1}{f} =  rac{1}{-30} -  rac{1}{-10}

The two negatives turn into a positive:
 rac{1}{f} =  rac{-1}{30} +  rac{1}{10}

To add these fractions, we need a common denominator, which is 30. Multiply top and bottom of the second fraction by 3:
 rac{1}{f} =  rac{-1}{30} +  rac{3}{30} =  rac{2}{30}

Simplify the fraction:
 rac{1}{f} =  rac{1}{15}

Flip both sides:
f = 15 cm.

The focal length of our magnifying glass is 15 cm. See how beautifully the algebra supports our real-world logic?

questions.Question: 18. Two immiscible liquids of refractive indices 8/5 and 3/2 are put in a beaker. Each column height is 6 cm. A coin is at the bottom. Find the apparent depth of the coin. Express as α/4 cm and find α.
questions.Hint: When you look through multiple layers of liquids, each layer lifts the image up a little bit. The total apparent depth is simply the sum of the apparent depths of each individual layer: d_total = d₁/μ₁ + d₂/μ₂.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Imagine looking down into a fancy cocktail or a jar of layered colored jellies. At the very bottom lies a shiny coin. You want to reach in and grab it, but light is playing a trick on you! Because light changes speed as it passes through each different jelly layer, it bends, making the coin appear lifted up toward the surface. 

The beauty of physics is that each layer acts independently to 'lift' the image. So, to find the total 'Apparent Depth' (how deep the coin actually looks from the top), we just calculate the apparent depth of each layer separately and add them together.

The formula for the apparent depth of a single layer is:
Apparent Depth =  rac{Real Depth}{\mu}
where μ is the refractive index (the 'bending power') of the liquid.

Let's look at Layer 1 (the bottom liquid):
Real depth (d₁) = 6 cm
Refractive index (μ₁) = 8/5
Apparent depth of layer 1 =  rac{6}{ rac{8}{5}}
When you divide by a fraction, you flip it and multiply:
= 6 	imes  rac{5}{8} =  rac{30}{8} cm.
Let's simplify that fraction by dividing top and bottom by 2:  rac{15}{4} cm.

Now let's look at Layer 2 (the top liquid):
Real depth (d₂) = 6 cm
Refractive index (μ₂) = 3/2
Apparent depth of layer 2 =  rac{6}{ rac{3}{2}}
Again, flip and multiply:
= 6 	imes  rac{2}{3} =  rac{12}{3} = 4 cm.

Now, we just add the apparent depths of both layers to see exactly where the coin seems to float:
Total Apparent Depth =  rac{15}{4} + 4
To add these, let's turn 4 into a fraction with 4 as the denominator:
4 =  rac{16}{4}

So, Total Apparent Depth =  rac{15}{4} +  rac{16}{4} =  rac{31}{4} cm.

The problem asks us to express this apparent depth in the format 'α/4' and find the value of α.
Comparing  rac{31}{4} to  rac{\alpha}{4}, it's obvious that α is simply 31. 

Each layer did its part to trick your eyes, but with a little fraction addition, you saw right through the illusion!

questions.Question: 19. The distance between object and its two times magnified real image produced by a convex lens is 45 cm. Find the focal length.
questions.Hint: A real image forms on the opposite side of the lens. Use the magnification formula (v = -2u) to relate object and image distance, then use the fact that the total physical distance between them is |u| + |v| = 45.
questions.Detailed_Explanation:
Think of a movie projector in a cinema. The film reel (the object) is inside the projector, light passes through a convex lens, and the movie (the real image) is projected onto the big screen far away. For a lens to project a real image onto a screen, the image must form on the OPPOSITE side of the lens from the object. This is a core rule of lenses.

Let's set up our coordinates. Let the lens sit at zero. The object is in front of the lens, so its distance (u) is a negative number.
The problem tells us the image is a 'two times magnified real image'. In physics, real images are always inverted (upside down), so the magnification (m) is negative:
m = -2.

The formula relating magnification, image distance (v), and object distance (u) is:
m =  rac{v}{u}
-2 =  rac{v}{u} \implies v = -2u.

Let's pause and make sure this makes sense. If u is negative (let's say -10), then v would be -2(-10) = +20. This means the image is on the positive side (opposite the object), which perfectly matches our movie projector logic!

Now, the problem tells us the physical gap between the object and the image is 45 cm. Since they are on opposite sides of the lens, the total distance is the distance from the object to the lens (|u|) plus the distance from the lens to the image (|v|).
Distance = |u| + |v| = 45.

Since v = -2u, the absolute distance |v| is exactly twice the absolute distance |u|:
|u| + 2|u| = 45
3|u| = 45
|u| = 15 cm.

Awesome! We now know exactly where everything is:
The object is 15 cm in front of the lens: u = -15 cm.
